steps.title: Set up: comparing data sets F and G
steps.content: Data set F contains 55 integers, all between 170 and 290. Data set G contains all 55 integers from F, plus the integer 10. So G has 56 integers total. Crucially, 10 is far smaller than every value in F.

steps.title: Test the mean: set up the inequality
steps.content:
Let $S$ = sum of the 55 integers in F. Then Mean(F) $= \frac{S}{55}$ and Mean(G) $= \frac{S + 10}{56}$.

For the mean of F to be less than the mean of G:
$$\frac{S}{55} \lt \frac{S + 10}{56}$$
Cross multiply: $56S \lt 55(S + 10)$, which simplifies to $56S \lt 55S + 550$, so $S \lt 550$.

However, every integer in F is at least 170, so $S \geq 55 \times 170 = 9350$. Since $S \geq 9350$, the inequality $S \lt 550$ is false. Therefore, Mean(F) $>$ Mean(G), not less.

steps.title: Test the median: position shift when adding the minimum
steps.content:
When F is sorted, the median is the 28th integer (call it $a_{28}$).

When G is sorted, 10 becomes the smallest value, so G looks like: $10, a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{55}$. With 56 integers, the median is the average of the 28th and 29th values. Counting positions: 10 is 1st, $a_1$ is 2nd,..., $a_{27}$ is 28th, $a_{28}$ is 29th.

So Median(G) $= \frac{a_{27} + a_{28}}{2}$.

Since the data is sorted in ascending order, $a_{27} \leq a_{28}$. Therefore: $$\frac{a_{27} + a_{28}}{2} \leq a_{28}$$
which means Median(G) $\leq$ Median(F). The median is NOT less for F.

steps.title: Conclusion: neither statistic must be less
steps.content: Both the mean and the median are greater for F than for G (or equal in the median case). Therefore, neither I nor II must be less for data set F than for data set G. That matches choice D — D is correct.
